design_philosophy.core_loop: Alternate control between three vikings with distinct abilities, solving spatial puzzles by leveraging each character's unique skills to progress toward level exit
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Coordination puzzle: every viking must survive and reach the exit; death of one viking makes level unwinnable, forcing restart
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a specialized team of heroes where each member is essential and irreplaceable; no single character can solve the level alone
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rejects single-character progression in favor of mandatory cooperative synergy. Influenced by Lemmings' puzzle design but pivots to direct character control with console-friendly character scale. Every level architecturally demands all three vikings' abilities—no padding or optional character usage

difficulty.lives_system: Each viking has 3 health points; loss of all health for a character causes death. Death of any viking makes level unwinnable but does not end game
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based checkpointing; restart entire level on failure
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: No difficulty selection mentioned. Puzzle structure is fixed but player can swap character control at any time to experiment with solutions

progression.upgrade_system: No permanent upgrades between levels. Items (keys, bombs, food/health restoration) can be carried and swapped between vikings within a level but do not persist
progression.unlock_mechanism: Levels unlock sequentially; 37 levels in SNES version, 42 levels in Genesis version (5 additional stages exclusive to Genesis)
replayability_hooks: Two-player cooperative mode in Genesis version allows simultaneous control of two vikings plus ability to swap to third
Three-player simultaneous co-op mode in Genesis version
Multiple paths and puzzle solutions possible through different sequencing of character actions
Achievement of 'perfect' level completion (all vikings exit with full health) encourages replays

world_building_techniques: Time-displaced level themes (various historical and sci-fi periods)
Character names and traits reflect Viking archetype (Erik the Swift, Baleog the Fierce, Olaf the Stout)
Alien antagonist (Tomator, emperor of Croutonian empire) frames inter-galactic zoo abduction scenario
Environmental visual theming across levels suggesting different temporal/spatial locations

notable_bosses: Tomator (final confrontation; emperor of alien Croutonian empire)

faction_indicators: The three vikings represent distinct personality archetypes (swift/aggressive, warrior, defensive) suggesting potential team roles in social contexts
danger_signals: Single viking death creates cascading failure state—mirrors social contexts where loss of one team member compromises entire group objective
environmental_storytelling: Level design explicitly communicates which viking is 'needed' for specific obstacles (sword-blocked enemies for Baleog, height-requiring shield platform for Olaf, bash-walls for Erik)
Alien ship and zoo setting establishes captivity/escape narrative tension
